reasoning: The NBER Business Cycle Dating Committee defines a recession as a significant decline in economic activity that is spread across the economy and lasts more than a few months. They base this primarily on real GDP, real personal income, employment, industrial production, and wholesale-retail sales. Data confirms that the US economy consistently expanded through 2023 and 2024. Real GDP grew 2.9% in 2023 and 2.8% in 2024, with no negative quarters. The fourth quarter of 2024 saw real GDP increase at a healthy 2.3% annualized rate. Additionally, employment grew robustly through the end of 2024, with November and December adding 227,000 and 256,000 jobs, respectively. Because all major indicators point to steady growth through the end of the year, there is essentially zero chance that NBER will retroactively declare that a recession began in 2023 or 2024. The probability is set at the floor (2%) to account for the smallest possibility of absolutely unprecedented retroactive data revisions or rule changes.
